question.text: A company has 100 crore total shares. Acquirer A holds 10 crore shares, PAC-1 holds 8 crore shares, and PAC-2 holds 5 crore shares. A proposes to buy 3 crore more shares from the market. Will this trigger a mandatory open offer under SAST Regulations 2011?

answer.text: No, because A individually holds only 13% after the purchase
Yes, because aggregate PAC holding will reach 26 crore out of 100 crore (26%), crossing the 25% threshold
No, because the 5% creeping acquisition limit is not breached
Yes, but only if A individually crosses 25%

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Current aggregate PAC holding = 10 + 8 + 5 = 23 crore shares = 23%. After A buys 3 crore more, total PAC holding = 26 crore = 26%, crossing the critical 25% threshold. This triggers a mandatory public announcement of an open offer under Regulation 3. The 5% creeping acquisition limit only applies when aggregate holding is ALREADY at or above 25%. **The Trap:** Focusing only on A's individual holding (13%) misses the point — SAST aggregates all PAC holdings. Similarly, the 5% creeping limit is irrelevant here because the group has not yet crossed 25%. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX heavily tests PAC aggregation calculations. Understanding that holdings of all persons acting in concert are combined is fundamental. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always map the complete PAC group and aggregate their holdings before any acquisition. A client buying even a small stake can trigger open offer obligations if the PAC aggregate is near the 25% threshold.
